div.p: Understanding horizontal reduction explains why attention is compute-bound

text.tspan: // Each attention score is a dot product
// Each attention score is a dot product

div.p: Softmax requires two horizontal reductions per row:

ol.li: find_max() to find max for numerical stability
hsum() to sum all exponentials These horizontal ops are the BOTTLENECK! Vertical exp/div are O(n) parallel. Horizontal sum is O(log n) tree reduction.

ul.li: d_k/4 SIMD multiply-adds → O(d_k) parallel work
1 horizontal reduction → O(log d_k) serialized For sequence length n:

n² horizontal reductions! The Insight from M3: Shuffle+add reduction is 2-3× faster than hadd_ps. This is why libraries like FlashAttention optimize the reduction pattern - it's the critical path. Memory bandwidth eventually dominates for large n, but for typical d_k=64, the reduction overhead is real.
O(n²) horizontal reductions Softmax adds O(n × seq_len) hsums

div.p: Every attention score needs one hsum Every softmax row needs two hsums Compiler often uses slow hadd_ps
shuffle+add pattern → 2-3× faster This is why FlashAttention exists Hand-tuned kernels beat auto-vec
Independent heads process in parallel No horizontal ops across heads Pure vertical SIMD - optimal!
